2026-06-19T09:03:58.6547279Z       "rationale": "enterprise/index.md has an explicit 'Section map' table listing every page in the section. lifecycle-hooks.md is absent from that table. An enterprise user landing on the enterprise index -- the primary entry point for platform engineers -- has no path to this feature except accidental search. This is the classic 'shipped but nobody knows' failure: the code and doc exist but the funnel is broken. Every conversion surface in the enterprise section (adoption playbook, governance guide) is a potential cross-link target that is also missing.",
2026-06-19T09:03:58.6558869Z       "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/index.md",
2026-06-19T09:04:00.9173619Z       "suggestion": "Add to the Section map table: '- [Lifecycle Hooks](./lifecycle-hooks/) -- fire shell commands or HTTPS webhooks on install, update, and uninstall; enterprise analytics and CI notification without modifying project configuration.' Also add a row to the 'Where to start' table for platform engineers."
2026-06-19T09:04:00.9175377Z     },
2026-06-19T09:04:00.9177123Z     {
2026-06-19T09:04:00.9177478Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:04:00.9203048Z       "summary": "CHANGELOG [Unreleased] has no entry -- existing users upgrading will not discover this feature",
2026-06-19T09:04:04.3169092Z       "rationale": "The [Unreleased] section of CHANGELOG.md is empty. Lifecycle hooks is a significant surface addition (new top-level CLI command group, new JSON schema, three discovery tiers, log file). Users who read the changelog before upgrading -- the segment most likely to evangelize -- will miss it entirely. Release narratives are mined from CHANGELOG; without an entry here the feature will not appear in the next release post. This directly undermines the 'existing user -> upgrades and shares' conversion funnel.",
2026-06-19T09:04:04.3173934Z       "file": "CHANGELOG.md",
2026-06-19T09:04:04.3175438Z       "line": 8,
2026-06-19T09:04:07.6599803Z       "suggestion": "Add under [Unreleased] Added: '- `apm hooks` command group: `apm hooks init` scaffolds a starter hook file, `apm hooks test [event]` dry-runs hooks without a real install, `apm hooks validate` checks all discovered files, and `apm hooks` lists active hooks. Hooks fire shell commands or HTTPS webhooks at pre/post install, update, and uninstall -- enterprise analytics, CI notifications, and custom automation with no project-config changes required for policy-level hooks. See [Lifecycle Hooks](https://microsoft.github.io/apm/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks/).'"
2026-06-19T09:04:07.6602127Z     },
2026-06-19T09:04:07.6602350Z     {
2026-06-19T09:04:07.6606677Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:04:07.6623188Z       "summary": "apm hooks init scaffold uses echo/cat -- first-run experience leaves value invisible",
2026-06-19T09:04:12.1716916Z       "rationale": "The default scaffold generated by apm hooks init produces a post-install command hook of `echo 'Installed:' && cat`. This is functionally correct (it shows the JSON payload on stdout) but strategically flat: a developer running apm hooks init sees nothing that makes them want to keep the hook. npm post-install hooks won mindshare because the first example was always 'run your build' or 'print a success message with color'. A two-entry default that shows (1) a working log-to-file command hook and (2) a commented-out HTTP webhook skeleton would immediately demonstrate both hook types and create the 'oh I see what this is for' moment that drives word-of-mouth.",
2026-06-19T09:04:12.1727447Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py",
2026-06-19T09:04:12.1735018Z       "line": 29,
2026-06-19T09:04:16.6746599Z       "suggestion": "Replace _INIT_TEMPLATE with a two-hook scaffold:\n{\n  \"version\": 1,\n  \"hooks\": {\n    \"post-install\": [\n      {\n        \"type\": \"command\",\n        \"bash\": \"echo \\\"[apm] post-install fired at $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)\\\" >> /tmp/apm-install.log\",\n        \"timeoutSec\": 5\n      }\n    ],\n    \"post-update\": [\n      {\n        \"_comment\": \"Uncomment to notify a Slack channel on update\",\n        \"_type\": \"http\",\n        \"_url\": \"https://hooks.slack.com/services/YOUR/SLACK/WEBHOOK\",\n        \"_timeoutSec\": 5\n      }\n    ]\n  }\n}\nThe log-to-file command runs immediately and leaves an artifact the user can verify with cat /tmp/apm-install.log, making the 'it worked' moment concrete. JSON does not support comments; the _comment/_type trick or a real second hook entry with a placeholder URL both work."
2026-06-19T09:04:16.6750652Z     },

The implementation in `_get_policy_hooks_dir()` returns `C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d` on Windows. Enterprise deployments often span Windows developer machines and CI runners. An enterprise reader provisioning policy hooks on Windows has no doc guidance.\n\nThe user hooks dir (`~/.apm/hooks/`) and the `APM_HOME` override are portable and correctly documented. Only the policy path is OS-specific and undocumented.",

2026-06-19T09:04:48.6637539Z       "title": "Hook examples use bash-only field without cross-platform guidance",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6644814Z       "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md",
2026-06-19T09:04:48.6653174Z       "lines": "56-72",
2026-06-19T09:04:53.1930947Z       "description": "The hook format examples in both the opening JSON block (line 38) and the Command type section (line 57) use the `bash` field only:\n```json\n{ \"type\": \"command\", \"bash\": \"./scripts/notify.sh\", \"timeoutSec\": 10 }\n```\n`effective_command` is `return self.bash or self.command` — `bash` always wins when set. On Windows (where bash may not be on PATH), a hook with only the `bash` field will attempt to run a bash invocation and fail. The doc correctly notes (line 68-69) that `command` is 'cross-platform' and `bash` takes priority, but the examples never show the dual-field pattern that makes a hook portable.\n\nEnterprise platform teams deploying policy hooks across mixed OS fleets need to know to supply both fields.",
2026-06-19T09:04:55.3661437Z       "suggested_fix": "Add a sentence in the Command section after the priority note (line 74), and update at least one example to show the dual-field pattern:\n\n```markdown\nFor hooks deployed to mixed OS environments, supply both fields:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"type\": \"command\",\n  \"bash\": \"./scripts/notify.sh\",\n  \"command\": \"powershell.exe -File scripts/notify.ps1\",\n  \"timeoutSec\": 10\n}\n```\n\n`bash` runs on Linux/macOS; `command` runs on Windows where `bash` is absent.\n```"

2026-06-19T09:05:07.7606995Z   "summary": "The hooks command group ships a solid skeleton — good event naming, guard on init, validate exit-code, sensible discovery order. Four issues need attention before this is safe to expose to end users. Two are blocking: the init scaffold produces a Windows-broken file that fails silently (first-:\n1. It does not state that hook execution occurs after APM's built-in `SecurityGate` has already scanned hook files for hidden Unicode and content findings — so the hook script itself is not APM-scanned.\n2. It does not note that `apm audit` does not scan hook contents or outputs (hooks are a separate execution surface).\n\nThis omission couldrun flow collapses), and apm hooks test is fundamentally broken for HTTP hooks because daemon threads are killed when the CLI exits, so the log the user is told to check is never written. Hook scripts and their output are not scanned by APM --\nadministrators are responsible for the trustworthiness of hook commands\ out the review.",

2026-06-19T09:05:10.2444554Z     },
2026-06-19T09:05:10.2445413Z     {
2026-06-19T09:05:10.2445968Z       "id": "doc-writer-7",
2026-06-19T09:05:10.2452202Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-19T09:05:10.3291034Z       "category": "structure(_INIT_TEMPLATE)",
2026-06-19T09:05:11.1249839Z       "description": "The template written by 'apm hooks init' contains only the 'bash' field: {\"bash\": \"echo 'Installed:' && cat\"}. On Windows, effective_command resolves to the bash value and runs it via subprocess.run(",
2026-06-19T09:05:11.1343325Z       "title": "Sidebar order 12 leaves unexplained gap; double-backtick heading style is inconsistent",
2026-06-19T09:05:11.1361655Z       "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md",
2026-06-19T09:05:11.1382856Z       "lines": "5, 205, 214, 226, 236",
2026-06-19T09:05:13.7502337Z       "description": "Two minor issues:\n\n1. Thecmd, shell=True) which uses cmd.exe. 'cat' is not a Windows built-in, so the hook fails. Because all hook failures are fire-and-forget-swallowed, the user sees '[+] event fired. Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output.' while the log highest existing sidebar order in the enterprise section is 9 (github-rulesets.md). Lifecycle hooks at order 12 skips 10 and 11. While Starlight tolerates gaps and the value doesn't cause a collision, a gap of 3 between the records status=error. A Windows developer following the canonical init → test flow gets a silent, undiagnosed broken state on the very previous max and this page will puzzle future contributors assigning orders to new pages.\n\n2. The CLI command headings use double-backtick formatting (e.g. `### ``apm hooks`` -- list discovered hooks`) which renders first command they run. The init scaffold is the first-run touchpoint; a broken scaffold is the worst possible first-run failure mode.",
2026-06-19T09:05:15.8188741Z       "suggested_fix": "Include both fields in the template so either as literal double backticks in most Markdown renderers rather than an inline code span. Other enterprise pages use single backticks in headings (e.g. `### \`apm policy status\``). The double-backtick pattern is unconventional and may render incorre platform works: {\"bash\": \"echo 'Installed:' && cat\", \"command\": \"echo Installed\"}. The runtime correctly prefers 'bash' on Unix and falls back to 'command' on Windows. Add a comment in the scaffold JSON: 'ctly.",
2026-06-19T09:05:16.0354409Z       "suggested_fix": "1. Change `order: 12` to `order: 10` to immediately follow github-rulesets.\n\n2. Replace double-backtick headings with single-backtick code spans:\n```\n### `apm hooks` — list discovered hooks\n### `apm hooks// bash: Unix/macOS  command: Windows/cross-platform' so the author understands the distinction without reading docs."
2026-06-19T09:05:16.0360609Z     },
2026-06-19T09:05:16.0361212Z     {
2026-06-19T09:05:16.0361726Z       "id": "DUX-2",

2026-06-19T09:05:20.5035568Z       "description": "HTTP hooks run in a daemon thread:Now I have all the evidence. Let me produce the return JSON. 'thread = threading.Thread(target=_send, daemon=True); thread.start()'. No join() is called anywhere. When 'apm hooks test' returns, the Click handler completes, the process exits, and the Python interpreter kills all remaining daemon threads — including the in-flight HTTP request. The log entry (_append_to_hook_log) is written inside _send(), so it is also killed. The success message 'Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output' points to a log that has nothing (or only stale entries). The user's primary workflow for verifying HTTP webhook connectivity — the entire stated purpose of 'apm hooks test' — is silently broken. This is not a UX polish issue; it is a functional correctness fault in the test command.",

2026-06-19T09:05:29.4641258Z       "description": "runner.fire('pre-install', event) is() blocks for sum(min(hook_ms, 30000ms)) per event phase. Worst case: 7 hooks * 30s * 2 phases (pre+post) = 420s = 7 minutes of serial subprocess wait added to a single install. Even called synchronously before the install pipeline. If a command hook is configured, subprocess.run() blocks the process for up to 30 seconds (default timeout) with no output, no progress indicator, and no hint that a hook is running. 2026-06-19T09:06:05.3301252Z       "title": "Empty-state 'Create one with: apm hooks init' is wrong for policy in containerized and cloud environments (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps, Kubernetes) where internal metadata services, sidecar APIs, and internal registries are reachable. Because HTTP hooks fire automaticallyC:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d is world-writable by default on Windows, enabling any local user to inject unskippable policy hooks",
2026-06-19T09:06:05.9567692Z       "rationale": "lifecycle_hooks.py line 220 resolves the policy hooks directory to C. For a typical apm install that takes 30s, a 10s-timeout HTTP hook thread will likely complete before process exit. But for a fast install and user hook sources",
2026-06-19T09:06:05.9590144Z       "location": "src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py:77, 237, hooks_validate",
2026-06-19T09:06:07.4185921Z       "description": "The empty-state hint 'Create one with: apm hooks init' appears in apm hooks list, a on every install/update/uninstall without user interaction, a project .apm/hooks.json can use hooks as an SSRF gadget to probe or interact with internal services on behalf of the CI runner. HTTPS-only enforcement does:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d on Windows. C:\\ProgramData inherits Authenticated Users: Write ACLs by default on most Windows configurations (the directory exists so all users can write application data, e.g. %ALLUSERSPROFILE%). This means any authenticated local user can create (all deps cached, <2s total wall-time) or when the HTTP endpoint is slow, the daemon thread is killed mid-request. From a perf perspective thispm hooks validate, and apm hooks test. apm hooks init creates a project-scope file (.apm/hooks.json). For a sysadmin who controls /etc/apm/policy.d/ or a user managing ~/. not mitigate this; it only closes the passive-MITM vector.",

2026-06-19T09:06:07.4195045Z       "line": 134,
2026-06-19T09:06:08.2289286Z       "suggestion": "After urlparse, resolve the hostname to an IP (socket.getaddrinfo) and reject if it falls C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d\\evil.json with arbitrary command or http hooks. A developer who clones a project with a .apm/hooks.json and runs apm install will have command hooks fire silently in the background with no per-install prompt or summary. This matches how npm postinstall attacks succeed: the attack surface is not just malicious authors but also developers who copy hooks.json templates without reading them. 2026-06-19T09:13:34.8426318Z {
2026-06-19T09:13:34.8432004Z   "persona": "python-architect",
2026-06-19T09:13:34.8443977Z   "active": true,
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5058474Z   "summary": "Solid foundation: clean dataclass value objects, correct fire-and-forget exception isolation, good JSON schema versioning. Three architectural issues compound over time: (1) build-runner + build-event + fire logic is triplicated across InstallService, update.py, and uninstall/cli.py -- adding a fourth operation copies it again; (2) command hooks block the CLI synchronously for up to 30s each while HTTP hooks are async, a discrepancy undocumented in the PR; (3) effective_command always prefers bash regardless of platform, breaking Windows enterprise targets. Pre-install hooks fire before the ImportError guard, leaving analytics systems with an orphaned event on the defensive path. Full URL including query-string credentials is written verbatim to hooks.log. Private _hooks access from the CLI layer creates coupling that resists future refactoring.",
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5061564Z   "findings": [
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5061795Z     {
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5066953Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:13:40.5075241Z       "summary": "Build-runner + build-event + fire logic triplicated across three files",
2026-06-19T09:13:51.6258366Z       "rationale": "InstallService._build_hook_runner/_build_event (service.py:113,129), _fire_update_hooks (update.py:615), and _fire_uninstall_hooks (uninstall/cli.py:313) each independently implement the same ~25-line pattern: resolve project_root from apm_package, call build_runner_from_context(), build a PackageInfo list, call LifecycleEvent.create(), call runner.fire(). The only variation is how the packages list is built (from get_apm_dependencies() vs a pre-built list). Adding a fourth operation type copies the pattern a fourth time.\n\nFix: add one free function to lifecycle_hooks.py:\n\n    def fire_event(\n        event_name: str,\n        *,\n        packages: list[PackageInfo],\n        scope: str,\n        project_root: str | None,\n        logger: Any = None,\n        verbose: bool = False,\n    ) -> None:\n        import contextlib\n        with contextlib.suppress(Exception):\n            runner = build_runner_from_context(logger=logger, verbose=verbose, project_root=project_root)\n            event = LifecycleEvent.create(event=event_name, packages=packages, scope=scope, working_directory=project_root)\n            runner.fire(event_name, event)\n\nAll three call sites reduce to: build the packages list, then call fire_event().\n\n**Design patterns**\n- Used in this PR: Dataclass-as-value-object (HookEntry, PackageInfo, LifecycleEvent -- appropriate and readable; consider adding frozen=True to LifecycleEvent since it crosses into the HTTP daemon thread); Strategy dispatch in execute_hook() (if/elif by hook_type, annotated as <<StrategyDispatch>> in the class diagram); Collect-then-render in discover_hooks() (accumulates from three sources before returning the full list).\n- Pragmatic suggestion: extract fire_event() free function to lifecycle_hooks.py -- collapses the triplication at exactly three call sites, the threshold where extraction pays off per the APM abstraction rule, without adding new abstraction layers.",
2026-06-19T09:13:51.6267879Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py",
2026-06-19T09:13:51.6268345Z       "line": 332,

2026-06-19T09:13:52.7140190Z     },

2026-06-19T09:13:52.7140860Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:13:53.8157698Z       "summary": "effective_command always prefers bash regardless of platform, breaking Windows",
2026-06-19T09:13:57.1489252Z       "rationale": "HookEntry.effective_command (lifecycle_hooks.py:143) returns self.bash or self.command unconditionally. A hook file that supplies both bash (Unix script) and command (Windows-safe fallback) will always run bash -- which fails silently on Windows without WSL. The enterprise policy hook use case (policy.d/*.json deployed to AD-managed Windows machines) is a primary motivation for this PR. _get_policy_hooks_dir() already has Windows-specific logic (lifecycle_hooks.py:219), confirming Windows is an intended target, making this a concrete cross-platform gap.",

2026-06-19T09:13:58.2503206Z     {
2026-06-19T09:13:58.2509522Z       "severity": "recommended",
2026-06-19T09:13:58.2538467Z       "summary": "Command hooks block the CLI synchronously up to 30s each; HTTP hooks do not -- undocumented contract mismatch",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6571980Z       "rationale": "_execute_command calls subprocess.run() at hook_executors.py:210, blocking the calling thread for up to 30s per hook (the default timeout). _execute_http fires a daemon thread (hook_executors.py:181) and returns immediately. With N command hooks on pre-install, the CLI blocks for N*30s before the install begins. The module docstring and PR body both describe all hooks as fire-and-forget, which is accurate only for HTTP. Enterprise pre-install use cases (analytics, CI gate callbacks) suggest command hooks should also be non-blocking unless the author explicitly intended serial pre-install gating.",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6576438Z       "file": "src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py",
2026-06-19T09:14:01.6577597Z       "line": 210,
2026-06-19T09:14:03.8526355Z       "suggestion": "Either (a) run _execute_command in a daemon thread matching _execute_http, (b) reduce the default command timeout to 10s to match HTTP, or (c) add a blocking: bool field to HookEntry (default False) and document the sync vs async contract explicitly in the schema."
2026-06-19T09:14:03.8528174Z     },

Analytics or CI systems consuming hook events will observe a pre-install event with no matching post-install. 2026-06-19T09:14:23.6923139Z     "diagrams": {
2026-06-19T09:14:28.9643724Z       "class_diagram": "classDiagram\n    direction LR\n    class HookEntry:::touched {\n        <<ValueObject>>\n        +hook_type str\n        +event str\n        +bash str\n        +command str\n        +url str\n        +source str\n        +effective_command() str\n        +effective_timeout() int\n    }\n    class PackageInfo {\n        <<ValueObject>>\n        +name str\n        +reference str\n    }\n    class LifecycleEvent:::touched {\n        <<ValueObject>>\n        +event str\n        +packages list\n        +scope str\n        +to_json() str\n        +create(event, pkgs) LifecycleEvent\n    }\n    class LifecycleHookRunner:::touched {\n        <<Orchestrator>>\n        -_hooks list[HookEntry]\n        -_logger CommandLogger\n        +fire(event_name, event) None\n    }\n    class hook_executors:::touched {\n        <<StrategyDispatch>>\n        +execute_hook(hook, event) None\n        -_execute_http() daemon Thread\n        -_execute_command() subprocess SYNC\n    }\n    class CommandLogger {\n        <<Interface>>\n        +verbose_detail(msg) None\n    }\n    class InstallService:::touched {\n        <<ApplicationService>>\n        +run(request) InstallResult\n        -_build_hook_runner(request)\n        -_build_event(event_name, request)\n    }\n    class update_py:::touched {\n        <<Module>>\n        -_fire_update_hooks()\n    }\n    class uninstall_cli:::touched {\n        <<Module>>\n        -_fire_uninstall_hooks()\n    }\n    note for hook_executors \"command: subprocess.run SYNC 30s / http: daemon Thread async\"\n    note for LifecycleHookRunner \"build+fire triplicated in InstallService, update_py, uninstall_cli\"\n    LifecycleHookRunner *-- HookEntry : holds\n    LifecycleHookRunner ..> hook_executors : dispatches\n    LifecycleHookRunner ..> CommandLogger : optional\n    LifecycleEvent *-- PackageInfo : carries\n    hook_executors ..> HookEntry : reads\n    hook_executors ..> LifecycleEvent : serialises\n    InstallService ..> LifecycleHookRunner : builds and fires\n    InstallService ..> LifecycleEvent : creates\n    update_py ..> LifecycleHookRunner : builds and fires\n    update_py ..> LifecycleEvent : creates\n    uninstall_cli ..> LifecycleHookRunner : builds and fires\n    uninstall_cli ..> LifecycleEvent : creates\n    classDef touched fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d47600",
2026-06-19T09:14:30.9772524Z       "component": "flowchart TD\n    A[apm install] --> B[InstallService.run]\n    B --> C[_build_hook_runner]\n    C --> D[\"[FS] discover_hooks lifecycle_hooks.py:252\"]\n    D --> E[\"[FS] policy.d and user hooks and .apm/hooks.json\"]\n    E --> F[LifecycleHookRunner]\n    F --> G[\"runner.fire pre-install service.py:70 BEFORE import guard\"]\n    G --> H[\"execute_hook loop hook_executors.py:86\"]\n    H --> I{hook_type}\n    I -->|command| J[\"[EXEC] subprocess.run BLOCKS 30s hook_executors.py:210\"]\n    I -->|http| K[\"[NET] Thread daemon async hook_executors.py:181\"]\n    J --> L[\"[FS] _append_to_hook_log full URL hook_executors.py:51\"]\n    K --> L\n    L --> M{more hooks}\n    M -->|yes| H\n    M -->|no| N{import run_install_pipeline}\n    N -->|ImportError| O[\"InstallNotAvailableError raised pre-install already fired\"]\n    N -->|ok| P[\"[NET+FS] run_install_pipeline install/pipeline.py\"]\n    P --> Q[\"runner.fire post-install service.py:106\"]\n    Q --> R[return InstallResult]",
2026-06-19T09:14:33.1414748Z       "sequence": "sequenceDiagram\n    participant CLI as apm install\n    participant SVC as InstallService\n    participant LHR as LifecycleHookRunner\n    participant EX as hook_executors\n    participant FS as filesystem\n    CLI->>SVC: run(request)\n    SVC->>LHR: discover_hooks + build runner\n    Note over LHR,FS: [FS] reads policy.d, ~/.apm/hooks, .apm/hooks.json\n    LHR-->>SVC: LifecycleHookRunner\n    Note over SVC: pre-install fires BEFORE import guard service.py:70\n    SVC->>LHR: fire(\"pre-install\", event)\n    LHR->>EX: execute_hook(command_hook)\n    Note over EX: [EXEC] subprocess.run BLOCKS up to 30s\n    EX->>FS: _append_to_hook_log(full_url)\n    FS-->>EX: done\n    EX-->>LHR: hook done\n    LHR-->>SVC: pre-install complete\n    SVC->>SVC: import run_install_pipeline\n    SVC->>SVC: run_install_pipeline(request)\n    SVC->>LHR: fire(\"post-install\", event)\n    LHR->>EX: execute_hook per hook\n    EX-->>LHR: done\n    LHR-->>SVC: post-install complete\n    SVC-->>CLI: InstallResult"

2026-06-19T09:17:25.9140163Z All 9 panelists returned. Now spawning CEO synthesizer.
2026-06-19T09:17:25.9140541Z 
2026-06-19T09:21:57.3273982Z ```json
2026-06-19T09:21:57.3277727Z {
2026-06-19T09:21:57.3301502Z   "headline": "Do not merge: _expand_env_vars is a zero-exploit PAT exfiltration channel, security.md now carries two false claims, and correctness bugs silently kill the feature's display layer and test command — all five must be fixed in-branch before this PR is enterprise-safe.",
2026-06-19T09:22:02.0277750Z   "arbitration": "Supply-chain-security and auth-expert independently converge on the same root finding, making it the highest-weight signal in this panel: _expand_env_vars resolves any $VAR from os.environ with no denylist, and _build_hook_env passes dict(os.environ) to every command-hook subprocess. A project's .apm/hooks.json can specify headers: {Authorization: \"$GITHUB_APM_PAT\"} and ship the live PAT to any HTTPS endpoint on every apm install — no exploit required, no user error required. The auth-expert's evidence block (TestExpandEnvVars::test_does_not_expand_github_apm_pat, outcome: missing, principles: [secure-by-default, governed-by-policy]) confirms no test enforces this contract today; per arbitration policy, a missing test on a secure-by-default surface ranks at blocking regardless of the persona's original tier. Supply-chain's additional finding that C:\\ProgramData\\APM\\policy.d is world-writable on Windows is a real policy-tier trust failure; I distinguish it from the cross-platform exfiltration path in calibration but do not defer it — an ACL-check stub with a tracked follow-up is the minimum acceptable; shipping without acknowledgment is not.\n\nThree correctness bugs mean the feature will not work correctly out of the box. CLI-logging-expert identifies a permanent import failure: from apm_cli.utils.console import console references a name that does not exist in console.py — the import always fails silently, the Rich table and Panel are permanently dead, and every user sees only the bare click.echo fallback. DevX-UX-expert identifies that apm hooks test exits before its HTTP daemon threads complete, meaning the primary test workflow silently produces no output in hooks.log. Most critically for enterprise trust, supply-chain's finding that security.md lines 32–33 now carry false claims ('No arbitrary code execution', 'No network calls after install') must be corrected in this PR — these claims are relied upon by enterprise security reviewers and allowing them to persist after merge is a public contract violation. Doc-writer's corroborating finding that 'fire-and-forget' is factually wrong for command hooks (which block subprocess.run() for up to 30 seconds each) must be corrected in the module docstring and the documentation in the same commit.\n\nPython-architect and performance-expert agree on structural concerns — triplication of fire logic across three files, serial hook execution adding N×30s worst-case per phase, 30s default timeout 3–6× too generous for a synchronous CLI path — that compound over time but are suitable for tracked follow-up issues once the five blockers are resolved. The oss-growth-hacker's 'APM's npm-scripts moment' framing is exactly right: the three-tier discovery model and apm hooks init && apm hooks test flow are genuinely differentiated. That growth upside is worth protecting — which is precisely why the five blocking items must land before this PR ships. A token-exfiltration incident in week one would permanently damage the enterprise trust that the hooks feature is intended to deepen.",
2026-06-19T09:22:03.0326183Z   "dissent_notes": "No panelists disagreed on the core findings. One calibration worth naming: supply-chain classified the Windows policy.d world-writable path as blocking alongside the cross-platform exfiltration findings. I distinguish them by blast radius — the exfiltration path (any platform, any project hook, zero user error) is categorically more urgent than the Windows-only local-privilege path. The Windows ACL finding is also blocking but could ship with an ownership-check warning stub and a tracked follow-up rather than holding the Linux/macOS user base indefinitely; I do not side with full deferral. All test-coverage-expert findings carry valid evidence blocks with outcome: missing; none are downweighted. The doc-writer's two blocking findings (false fire-and-forget claim, missing commands.md entry) were undisputed across all nine panelists and are included in the required rework scope.",
2026-06-19T09:22:03.0331899Z   "principle_alignment": {
2026-06-19T09:22:04.0744612Z     "secure_by_default": "Failed. _expand_env_vars expands any credential env var into HTTP hook headers with no denylist; _build_hook_env passes the full process environment including GITHUB_APM_PAT and ADO_APM_PAT to shell subprocesses. Both are zero-exploit paths: a project maintainer adds one line to hooks.json and every developer's live PAT is exfiltrated on install with no indicator. Auth-expert's evidence (outcome: missing, secure-by-default + governed-by-policy surfaces) confirms no test enforces this promise. Raw URLs including embedded user:password credentials are also written verbatim to hooks.log, bypassing SecretRedactionFilter. These are not hardening deferrals — they are pre-merge requirements.",
2026-06-19T09:22:05.0820276Z     "governed_by_policy": "Partially failed. The three-tier discovery model (user / project / policy) is architecturally sound and the right governance structure. The policy tier is undermined by the world-writable Windows policy directory, which allows any local user to inject hooks that run with the privileges of any apm user on that machine. The credential denylist gap means project-tier hooks can also circumvent governance by exfiltrating tokens before any policy gate fires. The trust model needs both the ACL check and the env-var denylist to be structurally meaningful.",
2026-06-19T09:22:06.0337194Z     "pragmatic_as_npm": "Partially failed. The feature skeleton — three-tier discovery, init scaffold, test command — is genuine npm-scripts-level ergonomics and the right UX direction. Broken by: command hooks blocking the install terminal for N×30s with no progress indicator; apm hooks test silently producing no log output because HTTP daemon threads are killed on CLI exit; the init scaffold generating a Windows-broken bash-only template; and the console import failure that renders the entire display layer as bare fallback text for every user. The npm-parity promise requires these to work correctly before launch."
2026-06-19T09:22:06.0366013Z   },
2026-06-19T09:22:08.0452810Z   "growth_amplification": "oss-growth-hacker's 'APM's npm-scripts moment' is the right launch narrative: lifecycle hooks close the last ergonomic gap between APM and npm for AI-stack CI/CD integrations, and the three-tier discovery model is a genuine differentiator incumbents cannot copy without breaking their existing hook contracts. The 'apm hooks init && apm hooks test' 15-second GIF conversion angle is strong, unused, and will convert better than prose. Hold the launch until the security fixes land — a token-exfiltration CVE in week one would permanently damage the enterprise trust this feature is meant to deepen. When fixed: add a CHANGELOG [Unreleased] entry framing hooks as 'zero-friction audit instrumentation'; cross-link from enterprise/adoption-playbook.md; seed a community hooks gallery in GitHub Discussions; add the npm-lifecycle analogy to the doc opening for JS-ecosystem onramp. 2026-06-19T09:22:17.0422197Z     "prose": "Five blocking items must land in the same commit before this PR merges. Security fixes: add a credential denylist to _expand_env_vars with a test asserting GITHUB_APM_PAT and ADO_APM_PAT cannot resolve into HTTP headers; replace dict(os.environ) in _build_hook_env with a minimal curated environment that strips all credential variables; update security.md to retract the two now-false security claims. Correctness fixes: resolve the console import so the Rich display layer works; add a thread drain to apm hooks test so the test command produces log output. Architecture improvements (serial hook execution, fire-logic triplication, 30s timeout, Windows ACL hardening, SSRF guard, URL redaction in hooks.log) are well-scoped and suitable as tracked follow-up issues on the same milestone — the doc-writer's commands.md gap and the oss-growth-hacker's CHANGELOG and index entries belong there too. The feature concept is strong: three-tier discovery is the right architecture, the init+test flow is genuine npm-parity ergonomics, and the growth upside is real. These are fixable issues measured in days, not weeks. Do not ship as-is to enterprise users who have staked their security posture on APM's documented promises."
